data.title: Newsom calls Trump's Davos speech 'remarkably boring'
data.description:
California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) on Wednesday dismissed President Trump’s speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, as “remarkably boring.”

“It was remarkably insignificant,” Newsom continued as he spoke with CNN’s Kaitlan Collins in Davos. “He was never going to invade Greenland, it was never real. So that was always a fake, and so he says, ‘We should negotiate.’ Everybody here has been willing to negotiate for a year.”

By “Trump standards,” Newsom said the speech made him feel “rather curious.” He said Trump’s escalating rhetoric over the U.S. acquiring Greenland was only “fire and fury [that] signified absolutely nothing.”

“And there were boorish parts of it but those were not even that consequential, including name-checking people he likes, people he didn’t like,” Newsom said. “So honestly, I was just a little disappointed. I was a little nonplussed.”

Collins asked if it stood out to the California Democrat when Trump referred to Greenland as “Iceland” during his speech. Newsom started spinning his finger in a circle.

“[It stood out] in that every time that a windmill turns, it costs a thousand dollars,” Newsom said. “A lot of stuff stands out. None of this is normalized. There’s a normalization, a deviancy of consciousness and comments and commentary. He’s ahead of the curve, he’s graded on the curve. That’s really some jaw-dropping and remarkable statements that just fly in the face of facts and evidence and common sense.”

Newsom added that there was “nothing new” about aspects of the speech, including the Greenland acquisition push.

“The only thing that was new was is he said he was willing to negotiate,” Newsom said. “But wait a second. That’s not new either. I guess he’s been saying we’ve wanted to negotiate for over a year.”

During his speech, Trump praised Newsom as a “good guy” while the California governor watched.

“We’re going to help the people in California,” Trump said. “We want to have no crime. I know Gavin is here. I used to get along so great with Gavin when I was president. Gavin’s a good guy. And if you needed it, we’d do it in a heartbeat. We did help them a lot in Los Angeles, a lot, with the early, early in my term when they had some problems.”

Like Newsom, another Democratic governor seen as a potential 2028 candidate for the Democratic presidential primary who attended the World Economic Forum was Kentucky Gov. Andy Beshear (D). He also slammed Trump’s speech and his comments over why the U.S. needs Greenland.

“The President’s address here in Davos is dangerous, disrespectful and unhinged,” he wrote in a statement he shared on the social platform X. “From insulting our allies to telling struggling Americans that he’s fixed inflation and the economy is amazing, the President is hurting both our families’ financial security and our national security.”

“Oh, and Greenland is so important he’s calling it Iceland,” Beshear added.

Trump did pull back threats he’s made in recent weeks over militarily taking over Greenland. He praised the island territory while calling for immediate negotiations and also accusing Denmark of being “ungrateful” over its support from the U.S. during World War II.

“Without us, you’d all be speaking German and a little Japanese, perhaps,” Trump said.

data.title: Bessent dismisses Denmark as 'irrelevant'
data.description:
Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent called Denmark “irrelevant” on Tuesday amid tensions between the U.S. and the Scandinavian country over Greenland.

“Denmark’s investment in U.S. Treasury bonds — like Denmark itself — is irrelevant,” Bessent said at a press conference Tuesday.

“Less than 100 million dollars, they’ve been selling Treasurys for years,” he added. “I’m not concerned at all. Again, as Treasury secretary, I see our Treasury auctions. We’ve had record foreign investment.”

A pension operator in Denmark, AkademikerPension, said it was withdrawing from U.S. Treasurys due to worries about finance, CNBC reported Tuesday. The operator told the outlet it had U.S. Treasurys totaling $100 million.

President Trump’s quest to acquire Greenland, which is a Danish territory, has rattled American foreign relations in recent weeks, especially with allies. The president has threatened tariffs over the situation, leading to pushback from fellow NATO members like the United Kingdom and Canada.

Throughout his first year of his second term, Trump’s tariff policy had already strained relationships and shook up markets across the globe.

Early Tuesday, stocks fell amid Trump continuing to push to acquire Greenland. The Dow had fallen close to 700 points by mid-morning, the S&P 500 dropped by over 1.4 percent and the Nasdaq dipped by more than 1.7 percent.

During a speech at the World Economic Forum on Wednesday, Trump also went after Denmark. He called the country “ungrateful” for American help in World War II.

“Without us, you’d all be speaking German and a little Japanese, perhaps,” the president said.

data.title: Trump admin drops court appeal blocking anti-DEI effort against universities, K-12 schools
data.description:
The Trump administration dropped its appeal of a court decision that blocked the implementation of two memos issued last year, which threatened to pull school funding over certain actions around diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI).  

The appeal upholds a ruling by the federal judge finding the letters violated the First Amendment and cannot be implemented. 

The Hill has reached out to the Education Department for comment. 

The letters sent to schools last February said if universities or K-12 institutions did not drop certain DEI measures, federal funding could be taken away, but it did not specify what counted as DEI initiatives.  

The one letter told universities race could not be used in decisions around admissions, hiring, scholarships and “all other aspects of student, academic, and campus life,” without more clarification.

The department sent a follow-up letter to universities as some were unsure if the guidance applied to student groups based on ethnicity and race.  

For K-12 schools, states were asked to sign a certification that it would abide by the memo, with many refusing to do so.

The case was originally brought by the American Federation of Teachers.  

“Today’s dismissal confirms what the data shows: government attorneys are having an increasingly difficult time defending the lawlessness of the president and his cabinet,” said Skye Perryman, president and CEO of Democracy Forward. 

“And, when people show up and resist, they win. This is a welcome relief and a meaningful win for public education.”

data.title: America's $10 donor recession: Why grassroots giving hasn't rebounded
data.description:
For years, small-dollar donations were the heartbeat of American politics. The $10, $20, and $50 contributions that once flooded ActBlue and WinRed powered a modern wave of grassroots activism, particularly during the 2016, 2018 and 2020 election cycles. In 2020 alone, Americans gave more than $4 billion in donations under $200, cementing the small donor as a dominant political force.  

But that era has stalled — and not just on one side of the aisle. 

Both Democratic and Republican campaigns are reporting a noticeable drop in small-dollar fundraising. Digital strategists now openly acknowledge a significant decline in low-dollar donations driven by email and text. 

Even President Trump, historically one of the strongest small-dollar fundraisers in modern politics, has seen his share of sub-$200 contributions fall from nearly half of his total in 2020 to less than one-third this cycle. Overall, his small-dollar fundraising is down roughly 40 percent from four years ago. 

So, what changed? Why are the most loyal grassroots donors — those who give out of passion rather than access — pulling back? 

The most compelling explanation isn’t political. It’s economic — or more precisely, perceptual. 

Small-dollar donations began slipping during the inflation surge of 2022 and 2023, when households faced sharp increases in groceries, gas, rent, insurance and utilities. As budgets tightened, discretionary spending was among the first things cut. Campaigns felt the impact immediately. Political donations, particularly small ones, proved highly sensitive to economic stress. 

What’s notable is what happened next.

Inflation has eased from its peak, and several macroeconomic indicators now show stability or improvement. Yet voter sentiment has not followed. Poll after poll shows Americans still feel financially strained, even as headline numbers move in a more positive direction. 

Consumer sentiment illustrates that gap clearly. The University of Michigan Index of Consumer Sentiment registered 50.3 in preliminary November readings — down from October and nearly 30 percent lower than a year earlier. The Conference Board’s Consumer Confidence Index remains stuck near the 90s, with its expectations component below 80 — a level traditionally associated with recession anxiety. That disconnect matters, because political giving is among the first behaviors to reflect economic unease. 

Fundraising data show a clear pattern: Voters have not returned to their pre-inflation donation habits. The sharpest decline is among the smallest donors. Micro donors — those giving under $100 — fell nearly 14 percent in 2023 and another 9 percent in 2024. Total political giving remains high, but that figure is increasingly sustained by larger donors who are less exposed to day-to-day price pressures. Campaigns see this firsthand. Large checks still arrive. The broad base does not. 

This is where perception becomes politically consequential. Voters may hear that the economy is improving, but that message competes with lived experience. Even as inflation cools, the memory of prolonged financial strain lingers — and many households still behave as if conditions remain uncertain. 

Grassroots donations are emotional rather than transactional. They reflect optimism, confidence and a sense of personal stability. When voters feel financially cautious — regardless of what economic indicators say — they are less likely to part with $20 for a campaign, even one they strongly support. 

In other words, we are in a transition phase. 

Political giving tends to move in cycles. Confidence fuels generosity — anxiety suppresses it. The most difficult period is not the downturn itself, but the space between downturn and recovery — when conditions are improving, yet confidence has not returned.

During the height of inflation, small donors pulled back sharply. Now, although pressures have eased, sentiment has not fully recovered, and donor behavior reflects that hesitation.

Historically, small-dollar fundraising surges when optimism is rising. When confidence stagnates or declines, grassroots giving retreats. During transitions between those states, donors wait, watch and hold back. 

That helps explain why the small-dollar engine that once powered American campaigns is running more cautiously today. This is not a collapse of engagement — voter interest in politics remains high. It is a recalibration shaped by prolonged economic unease. 

Whether small-dollar donations rebound in 2026 will depend on something no strategist can fully control: not just economic performance, but whether voters finally believe — and feel — that stability has returned. 

Until then, the small-dollar revolution appears to be entering a slower, more guarded phase.

Alexandra Kingston is president of Fundraising Inc., the nation’s largest multi-product fundraising firm. She is a former campaign staffer for Sens. Ted Cruz (R-Texas) and Tommy Tuberville (R-Ala.) and a former staffer on Capitol Hill.

data.title: These countries have agreed to join Trump's 'Board of Peace'
data.description:
Many countries have formally agreed to join President Trump’s Board of Peace to transform the Gaza strip into a stabilized region following disruption from conflict between Hamas and Israel, with Trump saying that as many as 59 nations are in play.

As the entities work to maintain a ceasefire authored by the Trump administration, international bodies are collaborating to restore relations in the Middle East. 

White House officials have invited 60 member nations to join the Board of Peace in an effort to ensure law and order is sustained and told countries it would cost $1 billion to secure a permanent seat on the board. 

Leaders are expected to solidify their decision to join the board during a Thursday signing ceremony in Davos, Switzerland, where foreign chiefs have gathered for the World Economic Forum.

While France, Norway and Sweden have already publicly declined their invitations citing concern for infringing on the United Nations, others have signed on to the agreement to work alongside Trump to quell violence in the region. 

Here are the countries that have signed on so far:

Argentina

“Argentina will always stand with countries that confront terrorism head on, defend life and property, and promote peace and freedom,” Argentine President Javier Milei wrote on the social platform X after accepting an invitation to join the board.

Belarus

“I have signed the appropriate appeal to the US, stating that we are ready to accept their offer and become founders of this council,” Belarusian President Alexander Lukashenko said on Tuesday during a news conference in Minsk, according to the state-run Anadolu Agency.

“What can we do there, helping Gaza too much? If we help, it won’t be much. But I’m attracted by the idea that maybe this Board of Peace, its actions and possibilities, will expand to other parts of the planet,” he said.

Morocco

“Welcoming the commitment and vision of President Donald Trump for promoting peace, His Majesty the King has graciously accepted this invitation. In this context, Morocco will ratify the founding charter of this Board,” Morocco’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs wrote in a statement.

“The Peace Board will take the legal form of an international organization with the aim of promoting stability, restoring governance, and ensuring lasting peace in areas affected by or threatened with conflict,” it continues.

It adds, “Its mandate will be based on practical cooperation, effective action, and result-oriented partnerships. Participation in this Board is strictly by invitation from the President of the United States of America.”

Vietnam

General Secretary of the Communist Party Tô Lâm accepted an invitation to join the Board of Peace on behalf of Vietnam on Tuesday.

He considers the establishment of the Board of Peace a necessary step to implement the Gaza Strip peace plan adopted by the United Nations Security Council under Resolution No. 2803 dated November 17, 2025, according to the Vietnam News Service. 

The resolution welcomes the participation of UN member states in the board to advance peacebuilding efforts, humanitarian assistance, and post-conflict reconstruction in the Gaza Strip, the outlet reported.

Pakistan

“In response to the invitation extended to Prime Minister Muhammad Shehbaz Sharif by the President of the United States, H.E. Donald J. Trump, Pakistan would like to announce its decision to join the Board of Peace (BoP) as part of its ongoing efforts to support the implementation of the Gaza Peace Plan under the framework of the United Nations Security Council Resolution 2803,” Pakistan’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs wrote in a statement. 

“Pakistan expresses the hope that with the creation of this framework, concrete steps will be taken towards the implementation of a permanent ceasefire, further scaling up of humanitarian aid for the Palestinians, as well as reconstruction of Gaza,” it added.

Kazakhstan

“On January 22, President Kassym-Jomart Tokayev will visit #Davos, at the invitation of U.S. President Donald Trump (@realDonaldTrump), to attend the signing ceremony of the Charter of the Board of Peace,” President Kassym-Jomart Tokayev’s office wrote in a Wednesday statement on X.

Uzbekistan

Uzbekistan is ready to join the Board of Peace as a “founding state,” said Sherzod Asadov, press secretary to President Shavkat Mirziyoyev, according to the Times of Central Asia. 

Mirziyoyev “noted that this initiative is seen as an important step in resolving long-standing conflicts in the Middle East and ensuring peace and stability in the vast region,” Asadov said this week. 

Kosovo

“True leadership doesn’t just talk about peace, it delivers it. That is exactly what @POTUS Trump is doing through this historic initiative,” Kosovo President Vjosa Osmani wrote on X.

“America helped bring peace to Kosovo. Today, Kosovo stands firmly as America’s ally, ready to help carry that peace forward. History remembers those who take bold steps to build peace — and we are ready,” she added.

Hungary 

Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orban, one of Trump’s strongest supporters in Europe, on Tuesday said he’d accepted the Trump administration’s invitation to join the Board of Peace.

“President Trump has invited Hungary to join the work of The Board of Peace as a founding member. We have, of course, accepted this honourable invitation,” the leader wrote in a post on X.

United Arab Emirates

“His Highness Sheikh Abdullah bin Zayed emphasized that the UAE’s decision reflects the importance of fully implementing President Donald J. Trump’s 20-point peace plan for Gaza, which is critical for the realization of the legitimate rights of the Palestinian people,” the UAE’s foreign ministry wrote in a Tuesday statement. 

“His Highness reaffirmed the UAE’s confidence in President Trump’s leadership and commitment to global peace, exemplified by the historic Abraham Accords.”

Egypt, Turkey, Qatar, Jordan, Indonesia and Saudi Arabia

In a joint statement, eight Middle Eastern countries agreed to join the Board of Peace.

“The Ministers announce their countries’ shared decision to join the Board of Peace. Each country will sign the joining documents according to their respective relevant legal and other necessary procedures, including the Arab Republic of Egypt, the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, and the United Arab Emirates, that have already announced to join,” they wrote in a collective statement posted by Qatar’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

“The Ministers reiterate their countries’ support for the peace efforts led by President Trump, and reaffirm their countries’ commitment to supporting the implementation of the mission of the Board of Peace as a transitional administration, as set out in the Comprehensive Plan to End the Gaza Conflict and endorsed by United Nations Security Council Resolution 2803, aimed at consolidating a permanent ceasefire, supporting the reconstruction of Gaza, and advancing a just and lasting peace grounded in the Palestinian right to self-determination and statehood in accordance with international law, thereby paving the way for security and stability for all countries and peoples of the region.”

Officials from Israel, Bahrain, Albania, Armenia and Azerbaijan have also signed on to join the Board of Peace.

data.title: Dimon: US 'less reliable' as economic partner under Trump
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase CEO Jamie Dimon said on Wednesday that the U.S. is “less reliable” as a global ally under President Trump, but he was otherwise reluctant to criticize the Trump during his interview with The Economist at the World Economic Forum.

Asked if he agrees with Canadian President Mark Carney’s assessment that there’s been a “rupture in the world order,” Dimon rejected the binary question.

“It’s not a rupture,” he said. “If you said to me, has America become unreliable? No. It’s just, you had total reliance, and now it’s less reliable.”

When The Economist editor-in-chief Zanny Minton Beddoes suggested that when being “less reliable, at some point, you become unreliable,” Dimon pushed back.

“We’re still a military ally to all 40 countries. When I talk to our military, they’re geared up to defend their allies around the world. You know, Trump hasn’t stopped all that. So, I just I, you know, I think it’s time for people to take a little bit of a deep breath,” the CEO said, adding, “That does not mean I like it all, you know.”

Dimon’s most adamant disagreement with the Trump administration came when he was asked about the president’s recent proposal to impose a 10 percent cap on credit card rates.

“It would be an economic disaster,” he said, noting that his company “would survive it” but “you have to have a drastic reduction of the credit card business. I mean drastic,” saying the move could remove credit from 80 percent of Americans.

He joked that two left-leaning states, Vermont and Massachusetts, should enact the credit card cap proposal first, saying advocates of the policy “will learn a real lesson.”

“And the people crying the most won’t be the credit card companies,” Dimon said. “It’ll be the restaurants, the retailers, the travel companies, the schools, the municipalities, because people will miss their water payments, their ‘this payment’ and ‘that payment.’ It would be, it would be something else to watch. I think they should test it.”

The CEO noted that he plans to present the Trump administration with more extensive analysis on the effects of this policy.

data.title: New Medicare policy may harm patients instead of fraudsters
data.description:
Access to quality health care while supporting free market competition is a bedrock principle of our health care system. That principle is especially important in Arizona’s 6th District, which is home to a large network of hospitals, rural health care providers, and an aging, retired population.

I am committed to advancing common-sense, bipartisan solutions that address the most pressing challenges facing our health care system. That’s why I’ve supported and helped introduce dozens of bipartisan bills to make our health care system work better for everyday Arizonans.

For example, I introduced legislation to help people with disabilities access Medicaid coverage. I supported a bill to expand research and preventive care for women facing lung cancer. I also backed legislation to give children better access to life-saving treatments. 

All of these efforts are focused on expanding care options for patients and encouraging new medical treatments — and they have bipartisan support. 

There is another imminent threat that must be addressed and that’s for treatment of senior citizens’ chronic wounds. On Jan. 1, 2026, Medicare’s 2026 Physician Fee Schedule took effect, greatly limiting access to chronic wound treatments and endangering patients, in the name of tackling fraud.   

Millions of Americans suffer from these hard-to-heal chronic wounds and require the use of skin substitutes, which physicians use to improve patient outcomes, especially in elderly patients. Many of these products are made from amniotic membranes and are highly effective in treating persistent and chronic wounds, such as diabetic foot ulcers, venous leg ulcers, and pressure wounds. 

These treatments don’t just help patients heal — they also save Medicare money. By preventing serious complications like infections or amputations, they save about $4,000 per patient each year. Without access to these treatments, many patients would face life-threatening infections or even lose limbs. In fact, experts estimate that a shortage could lead to hundreds of thousands of amputations and nearly 200,000 preventable deaths.

Access to these treatments is especially vital for my constituents, many of whom are at high-risk of diabetic foot ulcers and related complications. I represent nearly 80,000 veterans, 212,000 seniors and 8,000 Native Americans. Veterans and Native Americans have nearly twice the rate of diabetes as found in the general population, and about one-third of seniors have diabetes. An estimated 15 percent of diabetics develop a foot ulcer, with 6 percent subsequently hospitalized due to infection or other ulcer-related complications, including amputations. Mortality rates within one year of ulcer diagnosis are approximately 10 percent.

With thousands of my constituents at high-risk, I believe access to life-saving medical treatments must be preserved. Earlier this year, President Trump posted that he would not allow this access to lapse. He made good on that promise on Dec. 24 by withdrawing official local coverage determinations that threatened access to these treatments. I applaud the president for the decision and am committed to continuing my work on this issue and other bipartisan solutions to preserve access to this critically important treatment, especially now on the related physician fee schedule. 

Even with the withdrawal of the local coverage determinations that would have harmed my constituents, Medicare still must revise its latest physician fee schedule to ensure patients can still receive life-saving skin substitute treatments. Medicare should work with the Office of Inspector General to tackle fraud and abuse, but let’s not throw the good out with the bad by cutting access to affordable care for my most vulnerable constituents. 

Arizonans and Americans living with chronic wounds shouldn’t pay the price to address legitimate but narrowly focused problems. My focus is on patients and their families, and I’m committed to working with the administration and Congress to make sure they can continue to access skin substitutes and other critical treatments. For many patients, this care truly makes the difference. Lives and limbs depend on it. 

Juan Ciscomani represents Arizona’s 6th District in Congress.

data.title: House Democrat: Children in Greenland fear 'Americans might attack them'
data.description:
Rep. Madeleine Dean (D-Pa.) said Tuesday that President Trump’s renewed threats of annexing Greenland have left young children in the semi-autonomous territory frightened of a possible military attack, calling the president’s comments “nonsense.”

“The president is not making a lot of sense,” Dean, who sits on the House Foreign Affairs Committee, told host Erin Burnett on CNN’s “Erin Burnett OutFront.”

“He wants to possess things. The island of Greenland is not a possession. It’s not an asset for him. They are our ally,” she added.

Dean traveled to Denmark with a bipartisan congressional delegation this week, where they met with the Danish and Greenlandic prime ministers and parliament members.

“One of the most troubling things that we heard while we were there, from the Greenlandic members of Parliament, is that children in Greenland go to bed at night anxious, fearful, sleepless, worried that the Americans might attack them,” Dean said.

“What in God’s name are we doing? We have poured away — well, we haven’t, the president has — poured away respect and trust for our allies and, of course, respect in us. It is up to us, Congress, to reclaim that and tell the president that he is unwell in these ambitions.”

Trump has doubled down on his pressure campaign as he continues to position the strategically located island as vital to U.S. national security interests. He threatened over the weekend to slap 10 percent tariffs on Greenland and other European allies unless and until a deal for U.S. ownership is reached.  

Greenlandic and Danish officials have maintained that the island is not for sale and cautioned that any escalation could irreparably damage the longstanding NATO alliance.

“They’re steeled in their belief that this would be crossing an extraordinary red line,” Dean said of the countries’ leaders.

Greenland’s Prime Minister, Jens-Frederik Nielsen, said Tuesday that while the U.S. was “not likely” to use military force inside the country, an attack could not be ruled out, according to the New York Times.

Meanwhile, both Democratic and some Republican lawmakers on Capitol Hill are pushing back on the administration’s position.

Rep. Mike Lawler (R-N.Y.)  said Tuesday that there was “broad bipartisan opposition” in Congress to any kind of use of force in Greenland.

“If there was an opportunity to negotiate a sale, as in the past with Alaska or the Louisiana Purchase, this is not a new concept. Great. But the use of military force, in my opinion, should be off the table,” Lawler said during an appearance on CNN’s “The Situation Room.”

Dean said she’d spoken with Republican senators who agreed the potential use of military force was an “inane idea.”

“My question is: Why aren’t they speaking up now?” she asked rhetorically, concluding with a direct appeal to her GOP colleagues. “Go to the president and say: ‘This is a bad idea. You are actually in breach of trust of some of our most important allies.’”

Trump appeared to back off somewhat during a speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos on Wednesday, saying he wouldn’t take over using force.

“We probably won’t get anything unless I decide to use excessive strength and force where we would be frankly unstoppable. But, I won’t do that,” Trump said. “I don’t have to use force. I don’t want to use force. I won’t use force. All the United States is asking for is a place called Greenland.”

data.title: Beshear: Trump Davos speech 'dangerous, disrespectful and unhinged'
data.description:
Kentucky Gov. Andy Beshear (D) on Wednesday lambasted President Trump’s speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, which the governor called “dangerous, disrespectful and unhinged.”

Trump used his speech to make his pitch for why the U.S. should acquire Greenland, telling world leaders that he is not considering using the military to take the island territory after previously suggesting so.

“The President’s address here in Davos is dangerous, disrespectful and unhinged,” Beshear said in a statement he shared on the social platform X. “From insulting our allies to telling struggling Americans that he’s fixed inflation and the economy is amazing, the President is hurting both our families’ financial security and our national security.”

“Oh, and Greenland is so important he’s calling it Iceland,” Beshear wrote.

Trump praised Greenland in his speech in Davos, noting that he has “tremendous respect for the people of Greenland and Denmark,” but “the fact is no nation or group of nations is in any position to be able to secure Greenland, other than the United States.”

He called for immediate negotiations for the U.S. to acquire the island territory and backtracked on previous claims saying he would use military action to take Greenland. He has cited national security reasons as the basis for the U.S.’s need to acquire Greenland.

“We probably won’t get anything unless I decide to use excessive strength and force where we would be frankly unstoppable,” Trump said. “But I won’t do that. I don’t have to use force. I don’t want to use force. I won’t use force. All the United States is asking for is a place called Greenland.”

Trump accused Denmark of being “ungrateful” toward the U.S. after receiving help during World War II. He said Denmark would be “speaking German and a little Japanese, perhaps,” without the help of the U.S.

Beshear was not the only Democrat seen as a potential 2028 presidential candidate who weighed in on the president’s remarks. California Gov. Gavin Newsom (D) watched Trump talk about his state in person.

“We’re going to help the people in California,” Trump said. “We want to have no crime. I know Gavin is here. I used to get along so great with Gavin when I was president. Gavin’s a good guy. And if you needed it, we’d do it in a heartbeat. We did help them a lot in Los Angeles, a lot, with the early, early in my term when they had some problems.”

Newsom later told CNN’s Kaitlan Collins that Trump’s speech was “remarkably boring” and that Trump’s “fire and fury signified nothing.”

“It was remarkably insignificant,” Newsom added. “He was never going to invade Greenland, it was never real. So that was always a fake, and so he says, ‘We should negotiate.’ Everybody here has been willing to negotiate for a year.”

Newsom also criticized world leaders on Tuesday who he said have catered to Trump, saying he “should have brought kneepads.”

data.title: OpenAI rolls out age prediction technology for ChatGPT
data.description:
OpenAI said Tuesday that it’s rolling out age prediction technology for ChatGPT in the face of growing concerns about children’s safety when using AI chatbots.

The technology aims to estimate whether an account likely belongs to someone under 18 years old in order to ensure teens are using a version of ChatGPT with additional protections.

It will rely on a “combination of behavioral and account-level signals,” such as how long an account has existed, daily and longer term usage patterns and a user’s stated age.

“Deploying age prediction helps us learn which signals improve accuracy, and we use those learnings to continuously refine the model over time,” OpenAI noted in a blog post Tuesday.

Those under 18 years old will be directed to a version of ChatGPT that limits sensitive content, such as graphic violence, risky viral challenges, sexual or romantic role play, self-harm and extreme beauty standards or unhealthy dieting.

“While these content restrictions help reduce teens’ exposure to sensitive material, we are focused on continually improving these protections, especially to address attempts to bypass our safeguards,” the company added. “When we are not confident about someone’s age or have incomplete information, we default to a safer experience.”

Users who are incorrectly placed in the under-18 version of ChatGPT will be able to confirm their age using Persona, an identity verification software.

The push comes as AI chatbots across the board are facing growing scrutiny over how they interact with and impact young users. The family of 16-year-old Adam Raine sued OpenAI in August, alleging ChatGPT encouraged their son to take his own life.

Character.AI and Google, which are also facing several similar lawsuits, indicated in court filings earlier this month that they had reached settlements “in principle” with families in five cases.
